items.question: Who is responsible for choosing dtypes when loading a dataset?
items.answer: The engineer or data scientist writing the load step is responsible—they must specify dtypes, encodings, and columns rather than relying on blind inference, because downstream cleaning assumes the loaded frame is faithful.

items.question: What is the main difference between loading CSV and Parquet into pandas?
items.answer: CSV is text without embedded schema so pandas parses and infers types; Parquet is binary and columnar with stored types, allowing faster selective reads and more reliable dtypes via read_parquet.

items.question: When should you use usecols or columns at read time?
items.answer: Use them whenever the model or analysis needs only a subset of fields—especially on wide tables or large files—to reduce memory and I/O before any cleaning or joining steps.

items.question: Where does data leakage enter if you load the entire dataset at once?
items.answer: Leakage enters in the next preprocessing steps if you compute global statistics (means, category levels, scalers) on all loaded rows before isolating train, validation, and test partitions.

items.question: Why specify parse_dates when reading CSV timestamps?
items.answer: Parsing once at load produces datetime64 columns ready for sorting, filtering, and time-based splits; leaving dates as strings defers errors and breaks temporal ordering operations.

items.question: How do you estimate memory footprint immediately after loading?
items.answer: Call df.memory_usage(deep=True), sum the series, and inspect the largest columns; use the result to decide dtype downcasting, category conversion, or dropping unused columns before merges.

items.question: CSV vs Parquet for a 5 GB training table with 300 columns but 20 model features?
items.answer: Parquet wins: read_parquet with columns=[feature list] pulls only needed columns with native types. CSV would parse all 300 text columns unless usecols is set, wasting CPU and RAM.

items.question: object dtype vs category dtype for a country column with 180 unique values in 3 million rows?
items.answer: category stores one copy of each label plus integer codes per row—far less memory than object strings repeated millions of times; use category when cardinality is modest and labels are reused.

items.question: What if you load order_id as float64 and later merge on string IDs from another table?
items.answer: The merge fails or silently drops rows because 1001.0 never equals "1001"; you must reload with dtype string or astype before joining, wasting time on a full re-read.

items.question: What if you fill missing numeric values with the full-dataset mean right after read_csv?
items.answer: Validation and test rows influence the mean applied to training rows—a leakage pattern; split first and compute the mean on train only, then map to other partitions.
